items.q: Is a 'failed' experiment worthless in an experimental portfolio?
items.correct: No — documented unsuccessful experiments show learning and inform next steps.
items.misconception: Yes — only successful pieces should be kept.
items.random_wrong: Yes — failed work must be redone until perfect.
items.why: Students discard unsuccessful tests, losing the evidence of exploration and decision-making that the portfolio exists to show.

items.q: Does more annotation always mean a better portfolio?
items.correct: No — concise, purposeful notes linked to decisions matter more than quantity.
items.misconception: Yes — write as much as possible on every page.
items.random_wrong: No — portfolios must contain no writing at all.
items.why: Students pad pages with description instead of brief reflection on what they learned and what they will try next.

items.correct: First-hand recording: your own drawings, photographs and studies from observation.
items.misconception: Any image you find, as long as you redraw it yourself.
items.random_wrong: Only research carried out in a museum.
items.why: Students believe copying a found image converts it into primary research, but primary means recorded first-hand from life.

items.q: The main purpose of an experimental portfolio is to:
items.correct: Explore media, processes and ideas with breadth and ambition.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Explore media, processes and ideas with breadth and ambition.. “Practise handwriting for annotation.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is a primary source?
items.correct: An observational drawing you made of a market stall.
items.misconception: A scan from an art book.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: An observational drawing you made of a market stall.. “A scan from an art book.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Briefly explain decisions, discoveries and next steps.
items.misconception: Retell your whole day in detail.
items.random_wrong: Describe only the weather while you worked.
items.why: It's Briefly explain decisions, discoveries and next steps.. “Retell your whole day in detail.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Risk-taking is valued in experimental work because:
items.correct: Unexpected results can open new directions and show ambition.
items.misconception: It avoids the need for any drawing.
items.random_wrong: It removes the need to evaluate.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Unexpected results can open new directions and show ambition.. “It avoids the need for any drawing.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: You test 4 different media using 3 techniques each. How many experiments is that?
items.why: The real figure is 12 experiments. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
